narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid upper-middle-class family background, you've put significant effort into your law school application, including strong Year 1 and Year 3 decile rankings of 7 in your school, an LSAT score of 40, and plans to attend full-time. Unfortunately, the admissions model predicts a 72% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which is the key benchmark used to evaluate applications like yours. This high predicted risk led directly to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes candidates likely to pass the bar and succeed in practice. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your undergraduate GPA of 2.60, which falls below the typical 3.21 average and signaled potential challenges in handling rigorous legal academics to the model. Your family income bracket, while upper-middle and above average, surprisingly contributed in the same direction, perhaps reflecting patterns in the data where it correlates with other pressures. Your Year 1 decile of 7, higher than the average 5.71, also nudged the prediction toward failure in this context, despite seeming positive. On a brighter note, your Year 3 decile of 7 worked in your favor, pulling the odds toward passing since it exceeds the typical 5.53 average and shows later improvement. Your LSAT score of 40, well above the 36.70 average, and full-time enrollment status, shared by over 92% of students, had smaller positive leans but weren't enough to offset the top concerns. Compared to typical applicants, your test scores and later grades stand out favorably, but the lower undergrad GPA was the standout red flag that set you apart in a riskier light. To move forward, consider retaking undergrad-level courses or a post-baccalaureate program to boost your GPA, gathering stronger recommendation letters highlighting your resilience, or targeting schools with more holistic reviews that value your LSAT and family stability. You're clearly capable, and with targeted improvements, you can reshape this story for future applications—we're rooting for you.
